title: Trusts tackling inequalities as Black people almost four times more likely to be detained
summary: The NHS trusts' own membership body reports the headline disparity: Black people are almost four times more likely than white people to be detained under the Mental Health Act, and sets out what trusts are doing about it.
means: Your headline statistic for bids and commissioning conversations — from inside the NHS, so hard to dispute.

title: Monitoring the Mental Health Act in 2024 to 2025
summary: The regulator's annual report on how the Mental Health Act is being used — including the persistent ethnic disparities in detention, community treatment orders and restrictive practice.
means: The official record of the problem, updated annually. Cite the current edition so your evidence can't be dismissed as out of date.

title: Mental Health Bill receives Royal Assent
summary: The Mental Health Act reform became law in 2025 — the government's announcement sets out what changes, including measures intended to address racial disparities in detention.
means: The law has changed. Services and commissioners now have new duties you can hold them to — and new language for your proposals.

title: Mental Health Bill (England and Wales) receives Royal Assent — College response
summary: The professional body for psychiatrists responds to the new Act — useful for where the profession itself says implementation must go further.
means: When clinicians' own College concedes a point, you can build on it rather than argue it.

title: The Mental Health Act 2025: what you need to know
summary: A plain-English explainer of the new Act for NHS trust leaders — what changes, when, and what trusts must do differently.
means: Read what commissioners are being told, so you arrive knowing their obligations better than they do.

title: From mental health detention to health systems reform
summary: Peer-reviewed article framing disproportionate mental-health detention as a health-systems problem — reform of systems, not only services.
means: Academic backing for the position that community-level and system-level change belong in the same conversation.

title: Patient and Carer Race Equality Framework (PCREF)
summary: The national framework making race equality a contractual expectation for every NHS mental health trust — the first anti-racism framework embedded in NHS mental health policy.
means: The policy hook for almost any bid: your work helps trusts meet an obligation they already have.

title: Brief guide: Patient and Carer Race Equality Framework
summary: How the regulator inspects against PCREF — what inspectors look for when they assess a trust's race equality work.
means: Trusts are marked on this. Show a trust how partnership with you improves what the inspector sees.

title: PCREF at SLaM
summary: The South London trust's PCREF programme — one of the original pilot sites, covering Lambeth, Southwark, Lewisham and Croydon.
means: If you work in South London, this is your local trust's stated commitment — quote it back to them.

title: ELFT PCREF action plan
summary: East London's published PCREF action plan — concrete commitments with dates.
means: Published commitments are accountability handles. Check what was promised, then ask what happened.

title: PCREF at North East London NHS FT
summary: North East London's PCREF page and commitments.
means: Local accountability handle for organisations in North East London.

title: PCREF overview
summary: The East Midlands trust's PCREF overview document.
means: Accountability handle for the trust's East Midlands footprint.

title: Implementation of the Patient and Carer Race Equality Framework
summary: A Midlands trust's account of implementing PCREF.
means: Evidence that PCREF implementation is happening outside London — useful comparator for your own area.

title: PCREF action plan 2024–2027
summary: Sheffield's three-year PCREF action plan.
means: A multi-year published plan — the strongest kind of accountability handle.

title: Ethnicity Recording Improvement Plan
summary: The NHS plan to fix poor ethnicity recording in health data — without which disparities stay invisible.
means: When told 'the data doesn't show a problem', this is the NHS admitting the data itself is the problem.

title: NHS Race and Health Observatory
summary: The independent body reviewing ethnic inequalities in health and making recommendations to the NHS — its reviews cover mental health, maternity, workforce and more.
means: A standing source of NHS-commissioned evidence on race and health; check here before commissioning new research.

title: Ethnicity — ONS hub
summary: The national statistics hub for ethnicity data: population, census releases, and analysis.
means: Population denominators for your area and communities — the numbers every needs analysis starts from.

title: Sexual orientation, England and Wales: Census 2021
summary: The first census to ask about sexual orientation — national and local-authority-level estimates.
means: Official population estimates for LGB+ people, down to local level — no more 'we don't know how many'.

title: Gender identity by ethnic group — Census 2021 (RM038)
summary: Census cross-tabulation of gender identity by ethnicity — the intersectional population baseline.
means: The dataset that proves Black trans and gender-diverse people are countable — and counted.

title: Sexual orientation, further personal characteristics — Census 2021
summary: Census release crossing sexual orientation with ethnicity, religion and other characteristics.
means: The intersectional cross-tabs — sexual orientation by ethnicity — for population-level claims about Black queer communities.

title: Sexual orientation and gender identity quality information
summary: ONS's own assessment of how reliable the census sexual orientation and gender identity data is, and how to use it well.
means: Read before citing the census — knowing the caveats first beats having them used against you.

title: Health inequalities — ONS hub
summary: National statistics on health inequalities, including by ethnicity and deprivation.
means: Official inequality statistics to anchor a needs analysis.

title: Healthy life expectancy, UK
summary: How many years people live in good health, by area and deprivation — one of the starkest inequality measures published.
means: Healthy-life-expectancy gaps make inequality concrete for funders in a single number.

title: The Big Mental Health Report
summary: The annual report on the state of mental health in England and Wales — prevalence, services, funding and inequality — produced by Centre for Mental Health in collaboration with Mind.
means: The sector's flagship scale-of-need report for bids and briefings.

title: The Big Mental Health Report 2025
summary: Centre for Mental Health's edition of the annual sector stocktake, with economic analysis of mental ill-health.
means: The economic framing — costs of inaction — that Treasury-minded funders respond to.

title: Negative mental health effects of the rising cost of living
summary: Evidence on how the cost-of-living crisis is damaging mental health, naming people from Black and minority ethnic communities among the groups at highest risk.
means: Connects economic conditions to mental-health need — the context section of any current bid.

title: The health of people from ethnic minority groups in England
summary: An independent evidence review of ethnic health inequalities in England, across conditions and causes.
means: Independent, funder-recognised anchor for the needs analysis in any race-equity bid.

title: Quantifying health inequalities in England
summary: Analysis putting numbers on health inequality in England — who loses how many years, and to what.
means: When you need the inequality claim quantified rather than asserted.

title: UK health inequalities and life expectancy
summary: The doctors' union's evidence page on health inequalities and what medicine can and can't fix alone.
means: Clinical-establishment support for the position that health inequality is social, not just medical.

title: Black men's mental health — State of Care 2024–25
summary: The regulator's State of Care report gives Black men's mental health its own section — documenting how services fail Black men specifically.
means: The regulator naming Black men's experience as a system failure — cite it when a service claims the problem is engagement, not provision.

title: Improving mental health care for Black men — evidence brief
summary: A focused evidence brief on what works in mental health care for Black men.
means: A ready-made 'what works' summary for service design — check your model against it.

title: Men's mental health report — London
summary: The London Assembly's inquiry into men's mental health in the capital, with recommendations to the Mayor.
means: London-specific political recognition of men's mental health — leverage for London-based organisations.

title: Men's Health Strategy for England
summary: The first national men's health strategy — the government's stated priorities for men's physical and mental health.
means: A national strategy is a funding current: position your work as delivering it, and cite the sections that name racialised disparities.

title: Men's health: a strategic vision for England (CP 1432)
summary: The command paper setting out the strategic vision behind the Men's Health Strategy.
means: The full policy document — quote the government's own commitments back to commissioners.

title: Turning England's Men's Health Strategy into action
summary: Movember's analysis of what implementing the strategy would actually require.
means: A large men's-health funder signalling where it thinks delivery should go — read before approaching them.

title: The Friendship Bench — evidence profile
summary: Community grandmothers in Zimbabwe trained to deliver talking therapy on park benches — anxiety and depression significantly reduced in a randomised controlled trial, now adapted internationally.
means: The strongest single rebuttal to 'community approaches lack evidence': an RCT, at scale, community-delivered.

title: The role of Black-led organisations in supporting Black communities
summary: Documents what Black-led organisations actually do in mental health: trusted first contact, culturally grounded support, and advocacy the statutory system doesn't provide.
means: Names your organisation as infrastructure, not anecdote.

title: Culturally sensitive mental health support — partnership programme
summary: A major place-based funder's live programme increasing access to culturally sensitive mental-health support in Lambeth and Southwark.
means: Evidence that the culturally specific approach improves access — and a funder actively funding it in South London.

title: Beyond The Fade — barbershop mental health
summary: A Black-led community interest company supporting Black barbers and barbershop owners with their own mental health — care for the men who hold community spaces.
means: A working model of reaching Black men through the spaces they already own and trust.

title: Mentally Healthy Communities grants scheme
summary: A local-authority grants scheme funding community mental-health work in Birmingham.
means: An example of the local-authority funding route — and a live scheme if you work in Birmingham.

title: £11m project to improve mental health access in the Midlands
summary: A major funded research programme on improving access to mental-health care in the Midlands, including for racialised communities.
means: Large research money is moving on access inequality — a programme to engage with rather than duplicate.

title: Synergi Collaborative Centre — first phase
summary: The first phase of the national initiative on ethnic inequalities in severe mental illness, which ran to January 2022, built a knowledge hub of evidence briefings and systems-change work. That body of work is what this library draws on — later incarnations of Synergi are separate from it.
means: The evidence inheritance this platform builds on. Start here for the foundational UK work on ethnicity and severe mental illness.

title: The importance of cultural adaptation of psychological interventions (IAPT/CBT)
summary: Synergi briefing on why talking therapies need cultural adaptation to work equitably — and what adaptation means in practice.
means: The evidence for insisting that 'access to therapy' means therapy adapted to the people accessing it.

title: Queer and the Cost of Living Crisis — illustrated summary
summary: Research on how the cost-of-living crisis hits LGBTQ+ people, published as an illustrated plain-language summary as well as a paper.
means: Both evidence and a model: research outputs made for the community that provided the data. Set this as your accessibility bar for research partnerships.

title: ONS findings make it clear: urgent action needed
summary: LGBT Foundation's analysis of what ONS data show about LGBTQ+ inequality, and its call for action.
means: Community-sector interpretation of the official data — pairs with the ONS releases when you make the case.

title: The State of the Nation
summary: Terrence Higgins Trust's stocktake of HIV and STIs in England, documenting the unequal burden carried by particular communities.
means: Sexual-health inequality data to pair with mental-health evidence when making the whole-person case.

title: No More Silence
summary: NAZ's peer-support group for Black and mixed-heritage GBTQ+ men of African and Caribbean heritage — building wellbeing and knowledge around sexual health.
means: A Black and racialised-communities sexual-health organisation with decades of trust — a natural partner, not a competitor.

title: National LGBTQ+ mental health resources
summary: A directory of national mental-health resources, including LGBTQ+-specific services.
means: The referral map — where to signpost people while your own service is at capacity.

title: Sources of support
summary: TransActual's list of trans-inclusive organisations supporting trans people in the UK, with routes to searchable local directories (TranzWiki, Trans Unite).
means: The trans support map — part of building a service that meets people in full.
